yaml.title: Problem Set 1 - Solutions
yaml.subtitle:
Economics 100B - Intermediate Microeconomic Theory
  Prof. Muehlegger
yaml.author: Prof. Muehlegger

markdown.markdown:
a) **Derive the firm supply curve.**  
A competitive firm sets $MR=P$ and chooses $q$ to satisfy $P=MC(q)$ as long as it does not shut down.

$$
TC(q)=10q^2+90 \quad\Rightarrow\quad MC(q)=\frac{dTC}{dq}=20q.
$$

The shutdown condition is $P\ge \min AVC$. Here,
$$
AVC(q)=\frac{10q^2}{q}=10q,
$$
so $\min AVC=0$ (at $q\to 0$). Thus, for $P\ge 0$ the firm supplies where $P=20q$.

$$
q^*(P)=\frac{P}{20}\quad (P\ge 0).
$$

b) **Derive the market supply curve.**  
With 200 identical firms,
$$
Q_S(P)=200\,q^*(P)=200\cdot \frac{P}{20}=10P.
$$

c) **What are equilibrium price and equilibrium quantity?**  
Demand: $Q_D(P)=1500-5P$. Equilibrium solves $Q_S(P)=Q_D(P)$:
$$
10P=1500-5P \Rightarrow 15P=1500 \Rightarrow P^*=100.
$$
$$
Q^*=Q_S(100)=10\cdot 100=1000.
$$
Each firm produces $q^*=Q^*/200=1000/200=5$ (also $q^*=P/20=100/20=5$).

d) **Graph the inverse demand and inverse supply curves for the market the equilibrium price and quantity.**  
Inverse demand:
$$
Q=1500-5P \Rightarrow P=300-0.2Q.
$$
Inverse supply:
$$
Q=10P \Rightarrow P=\frac{Q}{10}.
$$
They intersect at $(Q^*,P^*)=(1000,100)$.

e) **Calculate consumer and producer surplus.**  
Consumer surplus is the triangle under demand above price:
$$
CS=\tfrac{1}{2}Q^*(P_{\text{choke}}-P^*)=\tfrac{1}{2}(1000)(300-100)=100{,}000.
$$
Producer surplus is the triangle above supply below price:
$$
PS=\tfrac{1}{2}Q^*(P^*-P_{\text{intercept}})=\tfrac{1}{2}(1000)(100-0)=50{,}000.
$$

f) **In the short-run, do the firms earn zero economic profits? Describe what will happen in the long-run.**  
Short-run profits per firm:
$$
\pi = Pq - TC(q)=100\cdot 5 - (10\cdot 5^2 + 90)=500-(250+90)=160>0.
$$
So firms earn **positive** economic profits in the short run.

In the long run, entry drives economic profits to zero. Average cost:
$$
AC(q)=\frac{TC(q)}{q}=10q+\frac{90}{q}.
$$
Minimize: $\frac{dAC}{dq}=10-\frac{90}{q^2}=0 \Rightarrow q=3$ and
$$
AC_{\min}=10\cdot 3 + \frac{90}{3}=60.
$$
Thus long-run price is $P^{LR}=60$. Then market quantity is
$$
Q^{LR}=1500-5\cdot 60=1200.
$$
Each firm produces $q^{LR}=P^{LR}/20=60/20=3$, so the long-run number of firms is
$$
N^{LR}=\frac{Q^{LR}}{q^{LR}}=\frac{1200}{3}=400.
$$

### Question 2: Monopoly

a) **What is the profit maximizing price for the monopoly?**  
First write inverse demand. From $Q=1500-5P$,
$$
P=300-0.2Q.
$$
Total revenue:
$$
TR(Q)=P\cdot Q=(300-0.2Q)Q=300Q-0.2Q^2,
$$
so marginal revenue:
$$
MR(Q)=\frac{dTR}{dQ}=300-0.4Q.
$$
Costs: $TC(Q)=\frac{1}{20}Q^2+18000=0.05Q^2+18000$, so
$$
MC(Q)=\frac{dTC}{dQ}=0.1Q.
$$
Set $MR=MC$:
$$
300-0.4Q=0.1Q \Rightarrow 300=0.5Q \Rightarrow Q_M=600.
$$
Price:
$$
P_M=300-0.2(600)=180.
$$

b) **What is the monopolistâ€™s profit?**  
$$
\pi = TR(Q_M)-TC(Q_M)=180\cdot 600 - \left(0.05\cdot 600^2 + 18000\right).
$$
Compute:
$$
TR=108{,}000,\quad 0.05\cdot 600^2=0.05\cdot 360{,}000=18{,}000,
$$
so $TC=18{,}000+18{,}000=36{,}000$ and
$$
\pi=108{,}000-36{,}000=72{,}000.
$$

c) **Draw the diagram for the monopoly.**  
Key objects to label:
- Demand: $P=300-0.2Q$
- MR: $MR=300-0.4Q$
- MC: $MC=0.1Q$
- Monopoly outcome: $Q_M=600$, $P_M=180$
- Profit rectangle: height $P_M-AC(Q_M)$ (or $P_M-AVC(Q_M)$ plus fixed cost) and width $Q_M$.

d) **Calculate consumer surplus, producer surplus, and deadweight loss.**  
Consumer surplus under monopoly:
$$
CS_M=\tfrac{1}{2}Q_M(P_{\text{choke}}-P_M)=\tfrac{1}{2}(600)(300-180)=36{,}000.
$$

Producer surplus is the area above MC and below price up to $Q_M$, i.e. $TR-\int_0^{Q_M} MC\,dQ$:
$$
\int_0^{Q_M} MC\,dQ=\int_0^{600}0.1Q\,dQ=0.05\cdot 600^2=18{,}000.
$$
So
$$
PS_M=TR-\int_0^{Q_M}MC\,dQ =108{,}000-18{,}000=90{,}000.
$$
(Notice that profits satisfy $\pi=PS_M-\text{Fixed Cost}=90{,}000-18{,}000=72{,}000$.)

To compute deadweight loss, compare to the competitive outcome where $P=MC$:
$$
300-0.2Q=0.1Q \Rightarrow 300=0.3Q \Rightarrow Q_C=1000,\quad P_C=100.
$$
DWL is the triangle between demand and MC over $Q\in[Q_M,Q_C]$:
Height at $Q_M$ is $P(Q_M)-MC(Q_M)=180-60=120$, base is $Q_C-Q_M=400$:
$$
DWL=\tfrac{1}{2}(400)(120)=24{,}000.
$$

e) **In the short-run, the monopoly earns positive economic profits. Will the monopoly continue to do so in the long-run? Why or why not?**  
If barriers to entry persist (the definition of monopoly), the firm can continue to earn positive economic profits in the long run. If entry becomes possible (e.g., patents expire, regulation changes, or substitutes enter), competition will erode market power and economic profits.
